--- SOURCE 4: Cutting-edge techniques and environmental insights in recycled ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403

SUMMARY:
Recycled concrete aggregates (RCA) offer a sustainable alternative to natural aggregates (NA), helping reduce landfill use and energy consumption by up to 85 % and lowering CO 2 emissions by 90 %, providing significant environmental and economic benefits. Recycled concrete aggregate (RCA) provides an effective alternative to NA, reducing environmental impacts and enhancing resource efficiency (Hansen, 1986). Studies have shown that recycled aggregates (RA) may be limited in their applicability to structural concrete due to higher water absorption, increased porosity, lower density, and diminished mechanical properties (Ryu, 2002, Marinković and Carević, 2018, Kapoor and Bohroo, 2019, Xiao et al., 2005, Butler et al., 2011, Rao et al., 2011).

FULL CONTENT:

Cutting-edge techniques and environmental insights in recycled concrete aggregate production: A comprehensive review
====================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Ram Prasad Neupane a, Ningombam Reena Devi b, Thanongsak Imjai a, Abhishek Rajput c, Takafumi Noguchi d

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rcradv.2024.200241](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rcradv.2024.200241 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2667378924000403&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Innovative RCA Production Techniques are reported and their advantages and limitations. 
*   •Introduces advanced techniques like carbonization, silica gel treatment, and bio-deposition, improving RCA compressive strength by 42 % and reducing water absorption by 27 %. 
*   •Compares various RCA treatment methods, highlighting significant performance enhancements and the need for standardized production and testing guidelines. 
*   •RCA use reduces energy consumption by 85 % and CO2 emissions by 90 %, promoting sustainable construction practices. 

Abstract
--------

The rapid growth of urban infrastructure has led to a substantial rise in construction and demolition waste (C&DW), causing environmental issues like overflowing landfills and depletion of natural resources. Recycled concrete aggregates (RCA) offer a sustainable alternative to natural aggregates (NA), helping reduce landfill use and energy consumption by up to 85 % and lowering CO 2 emissions by 90 %, providing significant environmental and economic benefits. However, untreated RCA exhibits lower strength and durability due to adhered mortar and impurities. This review delves into novel and advanced RCA treatment methods and production techniques that enhance RCA's properties. These methods achieve up to 42 % improvement in compressive strength and a 27 % reduction in water absorption. Researchers have found that impact crushers produce higher-quality aggregates compared to jaw or cone crushers. Treatments like acid washing and carbonation enhance particle density and reduce water absorption by 17.5 %. Recycled aggregate concrete (RAC) produced from treated RCA can reach 87 % of the compressive strength of natural aggregate concrete (NAC), with certain combined treatments achieving results close to NAC. Inconsistencies in RCA properties due to various treatments emphasise the need for standardised production and testing guidelines. This review underscores RCA's role in promoting sustainable construction and its potential to significantly mitigate CO 2 emissions, encouraging further research to refine these methods for long-term environmental gains.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000385)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000397)

Keywords
--------

Recycled concrete aggregates (RCA)

Recycled aggregate concrete (RAC)

Construction and demolition waste (C&DW)

Sustainable construction

Advanced treatment methods

Green materials

1. Introduction
---------------

Over the past two decades, urban population growth and infrastructure demands have driven extensive global urban redevelopment, resulting in a continuous cycle of construction and demolition. This redevelopment produces approximately 3 billion tonnes of construction and demolition waste (C&DW) annually, with concrete comprising a significant portion ([Akhtar and Sarmah, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0001)). Concrete is extensively used in structural and non-structural applications due to its low cost, wide application, formability, and high strength. However, rapid urbanisation has increased aggregate demand by 5.2 % every five years ([Neupane et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0047)), with Asian countries consumei about 62 % of the global total, including 38 % by China and 13 % by India ([Tam et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0002)). This significant demand for natural aggregate (NA) highlights the urgent need for sustainable alternatives in the construction industry. Landfill waste is projected to increase environmental impacts by 20.2 % in 2025. Recycling C&DW can significantly reduce these impacts. Studies indicate that recycling 50 % of C&DW can reduce environmental impacts by 33.2 %, while a 100 % recycling approach could achieve up to a 46.0 % reduction ([Mah et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0003)). This highlights the critical importance of adopting sustainable waste management practices.

Countries such as China, India, Indonesia, and Malaysia have significantly increased their recycling efforts, illustrating a global trend towards sustainable practices. The construction industry is highly dependent on natural resources, with global aggregate production nearly doubling from 21 billion tons in 2007 to 40 billion tons in 2014. This growth shows the urgent need for sustainable methods to manage waste, improve durability, save resources, and reduce energy use ([Behera et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0004), [Oikonomou, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0005), [Ugwu and Haupt, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0006)). Recycled concrete aggregate (RCA) provides an effective alternative to NA, reducing environmental impacts and enhancing resource efficiency ([Hansen, 1986](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0007)).

Recycling C&DW into RCA offers significant benefits, but it also presents challenges in terms of the engineering properties and quality of the recycled materials. Studies have shown that recycled aggregates (RA) may be limited in their applicability to structural concrete due to higher water absorption, increased porosity, lower density, and diminished mechanical properties ([Ryu, 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0008), [Marinković and Carević, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0009), [Kapoor and Bohroo, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0010), [Xiao et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0011), [Butler et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0012), [Rao et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0013)). Natural aggregate concrete (NAC) is generally more durable and mechanically stronger. Recycled aggregate concrete (RAC) needs about 5 % more cement to match the workability and strength of NAC ([Zega et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0014)). RAC usually has 25 % reduced compressive strength and 10 % lower tensile strength than NAC. It also experiences 25 % increased shrinkage and 30 % more creep strain ([Cabo et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0015), [Yehia et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0016)). The most significant difference between RAC and NAC is their absorption capacity ([Xiao et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0011), [Butler et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0012), [Rao et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0013)). Recycled aggregates often have lower density and strength because of the porous nature of the attached mortar ([Zhang et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0017), [Wang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0018)). Despite RCA's benefits, its use in construction is limited. This is due to the limited number of production plants, high setup costs, processing challenges, and quality control issues. RAC has drawbacks like lower strength, high porosity, more water absorption, greater shrinkage, creep, and durability issues. Regulatory and Standards limitations add to the challenges.

Improving RAC quality involves strengthening or removing the adhered mortar on RCA surfaces ([Vieira et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0019), [Juan and Gutiérrez, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0020)). Recent studies on advanced recycling and treatment methods have shown promising improvements in RAC strength and durability ([Oikonomou, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0005), [Ugwu and Haupt, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0006), [Tam and Tam, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0021), [Katz, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0022), [Tam et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0023)). Several treatment methods for RCA are already available in the construction industry, including acid treatment, thermal treatment, and mechanical treatment, which can improve the quality of RCA ([Kim, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0024), [Bahraq et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0025), [Tam and Tam, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0026), [Saravanakumar et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0027), [Wang et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0028), [Parthiban and Mohan, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0029), [Ismail and Ramli, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0030), [Imjai et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0031), [Setkit et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0032), [Leelatanon et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0033), [Imjai et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0034), [Taboada et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0035), [Ulsen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0036), [Akbarnezhad et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0037), [Kim et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0038)). Repurposing demolished buildings for housing using RAC technology can address its slightly lower quality than conventional concrete. However, treatments and additives can improve its performance ([Kisku et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0039)). Researchers are improving treatment techniques for RCA to boost its physical and mechanical properties and increase construction sustainability. However, RCA properties differ widely by region ([Marinković and Carević, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0009), [Ulsen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0036), [Padmini et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0040), [Savva et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0041), [Pedro et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0042), [Hubert et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0043), [Purushothaman et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0044)). Numerous review articles have recently focused on recycled aggregates, including Danish & Mosaberpanah ([Danish and Mosaberpanah, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0045)), which reviewed RCA production and properties and promoted its use in sustainable RAC. Kumar and Singh ([Kumar and Singh, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0046)) called for improving RCA properties through treatments to align RAC with NAC. [Neupane et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0047) highlighted challenges in RCA use in Southeast Asia and the need for standardisation. [Kumar and Singh (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0048) classified RCA into four quality categories, showing a strong correlation between RCA quality and RAC performance, making high-quality RCA comparable to NAC. [Lu et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0049) explored graphene oxide's role in enhancing RAC performance. [Ouyang et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0050) reviewed RCA pretreatment methods to improve RAC durability. [Bahraq et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0025) analysed durability issues and RA treatment methods. [Ma et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0051) identified key factors affecting recycled concrete pricing. [Wu et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0052) emphasised the limitations of large-scale RAC applications due to transport distance and RA replacement rates. [Hasheminezhad et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0053) compared RAC and NAC via life cycle assessment (LCA), highlighting environmental impact factors such as transport and strength reliability.

Inconsistencies in the published literature and limited standard specifications for RCA testing and design complicate its widespread application. This study comprehensively analyses RCA production processes, environmental impacts, and property changes from different crushing methods. It also identifies the most effective method that balances environmental impacts with material performance. Additionally, the study reviews various RCA treatment techniques and explores new ways to enhance their use in construction and promote sustainability.

2. Research significance
------------------------

RCA offers a promising solution for repurposing C&DW in sustainable construction materials. While there are standardised requirements for recycling and applying RCA in various countries, considerable variances exist between these Standards. Globally recognised Standards for RCA recycling and application are compiled and discussed in this study. The study aims to fill this gap by comprehensively reviewing RCA production processes and treatment methods to understand optimal processing techniques that balance environmental impacts with material performance. By evaluating the influence of different crushing methods and exploring innovative treatments, this research aims to establish a solid framework for standardised Guidelines and serve as a crucial resource for the research community. It aims to promote sustainable practices and provide valuable insights for enhancing the quality and reliability of RCA in construction.

3. Statistics of published works
--------------------------------

Recycling concrete aggregate and its application as a construction material is not a recent innovation. According to Buck ([Buck, 1976](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0054)), RCA was first used after World War II in the 1940s due to the significant amount of C&DW generated and the urgent need to clear the debris to rebuild Europe. After the initial need to recycle concrete was met, the use of RCA gradually decreased. In the 1970s, the United States reintroduced using RCA for low-grade purposes like base layers, foundation fills, and other non-structural purposes. When the People's Republic of China was founded in 1949, there was no advanced research on construction solid waste management. However, after 2000, China experienced rapid development and significant progress. A practical approach in construction solid waste (CSW) is to produce RCA by crushing, cleaning, sorting and processing the waste ([Zhan et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0055)). In the 1980s, developed countries began efforts to reduce and recycle construction waste. Germany started the Federal Quality Association for Recycled Building Materials in 1984, later becoming the European Quality Association for Recycling in 2006. Since then, significant progress has been made in managing construction waste, especially in Australia, Western Europe, and North America ([Tam et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#bib0002)). In recent years, the Asia/Pacific region, Russia, and South America have become top producers and sellers of aggregates due to booming construction, especially in China, India, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, the Gulf States, Turkey, Russia, Brazil, and Mexico. China alone drops half of the global increase in aggregate demand. As a result, many researchers have investigated using RCA as an alternative to natural aggregates in structural concrete. Over the past two decades, the use of RCA has grown in developing countries like China and India but is mostly limited to urban areas. RCA is seen as a promising sustainable material that helps reduce environmental impact. Despite many studies on RCA, there have been about 2624 publications in the Scopus database over the last 30 years. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#fig0001)**(a)** summarises key research conducted since 1996 based on available publications. The data indicates that studies in the field of RCA were minimal until 2010. Subsequently, interest in this topic increases significantly due to the increasing demand for sustainable RCA construction materials. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#fig0001)**(b)** presents a list of the top 10 countries in RCA research based on the number of publications available. The publications are further classified into articles, conference papers, and review articles to understand the research direction better. It is observed that articles constitute the majority of RCA publications (80 %), followed by conference papers (15 %), review papers (3 %), and books (2 %). [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#fig0002) shows a graphical depiction of the publication types**.** China and India have recently demonstrated that RCA can be a sustainable construction material. While its use is mostly confined to urban areas, research and application have grown significantly, especially in developing countries. Bibliometric analysis reveals a significant increase in RCA research since 2010, with China and India making major contributions.

A review of the literature shows that RCA research falls into five main areas: (1) the physical and mechanical properties of RCA, (2) recycling processes and production methods, (3) treatment techniques, (4) sustainable recycling practices and their economic and environmental effects, and (5) challenges in using RCA for structural applications. This review summarises current knowledge, highlights ongoing research trends, and underscores the need for more innovation and standardisation to improve RCA as a sustainable construction material.

4. Physico-mechanical properties
--------------------------------

Construction materials' physical and mechanical properties greatly affect their selection and performance. Many materials show significant regional differences due to varying molecular structures and production and treatment methods inconsistencies. [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667378924000403#tbl0001) shows that RCA has a wide range of physical and mechanical properties in the literature. enriched_points.expanded_content: Recycled aggregates (RCA) derived from construction debris significantly reduce the need for natural aggregates, promoting resource efficiency and minimizing landfill waste.
Utilizing recycled aggregates in concrete can lower CO2 emissions by up to 90%, contributing to a reduced carbon footprint compared to traditional natural aggregates.
Concrete mixes with 100% recycled aggregates and supplementary cementitious materials, like fly ash, demonstrate lower carbon emissions and comparable strength to conventional concrete.
